hypothesis: Reduce the emergency_multiplier from 1.5 to 1.2 to make emergency orders less aggressive and potentially reduce excess inventory.

To reduce excess inventory without crashing the fill rate, we will implement a change to the order quantity calculation by rounding down the order quantity to the nearest 5, which should result in fewer small top-up orders and potentially reduce excess inventory.

Reducing the ORDER_HORIZON from 10 to 8 and increasing the SAFETY_FACTOR to 1.15 will allow the policy to be less aggressive in ordering while maintaining a high fill rate and reducing excess inventory.

Implementing a conditional safety factor based on product volume will reduce excess inventory by being less conservative for high-volume products and more conservative for low-volume products, thus improving the overall score.
To reduce excess inventory without crashing the fill rate, we will implement a cap on the maximum order quantity to prevent huge single orders that may lead to excess inventory.
Implementing a day-of-week bias to order 15% less on Friday, as weekend demand is typically lower for most retail, will help reduce excess inventory without crashing the fill rate.

We will implement a tighter trigger for reordering by only reordering when the current inventory is less than 90% of the reorder point, which should help reduce excess inventory without crashing the fill rate.
Reducing the ORDER_HORIZON from 10 to 9 will allow the policy to be less aggressive in ordering while maintaining a high fill rate and reducing excess inventory.

Implementing a cap on the maximum order quantity will prevent huge single orders that may lead to excess inventory, thus improving the overall score by reducing excess inventory without crashing the fill rate.

To reduce excess inventory without crashing the fill rate, we will implement an inventory velocity check that skips reordering if the inventory is increasing over the last 3 days, indicating a potential drop in demand.
